summary:
The Fall of Gojoseon (109–108 BC) was the [[event:77|Han Dynasty]] military conquest of the [[event:1166|Gojoseon Kingdom]] under [[event:1127|Emperor Wu of Han]], which ended Korea's first historical kingdom and established four Han commanderies on the Korean peninsula, integrating Korea into the Chinese cultural sphere for the first time.

The campaign followed years of tension as Gojoseon's king Ugeo resisted Han demands that he allow free passage of ambassadors and subordinate himself more fully to Han overlordship. Emperor Wu sent both naval forces across the Yellow Sea and land forces through Manchuria. The campaign was not a quick victory — Gojoseon resisted for over a year, and Han forces suffered significant setbacks including mutiny. Ultimately internal divisions among the Korean leadership led to Ugeo's assassination by his own ministers, who surrendered to Han. Four commanderies (Lelang, Xuantu, Lintun, and Zhenfan) were established. Of these, Lelang (near modern Pyongyang) survived until 313 AD and became a significant center of Chinese culture and administration on the peninsula, through which Chinese writing, Confucianism, Buddhism, and material culture reached the emerging Korean kingdoms.
